title: Apply: magnetic field due to straight current-carrying conductors
description: Apply your knowledge of magnetic fields due to straight current-carrying conductors in this set of free practice questions.
topic_path: Apply: magnetic field due to straight current-carrying conductors

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current flows through a straight conductor, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which model correctly shows the shape of the magnetic field lines produced around the conductor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The magnetic field lines around a current-carrying wire form concentric circles *around* the conductor.

The direction of the magnetic field lines is determined using the *right hand rule*. 

If you point the thumb of your *right hand* in the direction of the current (up), your four fingers wrap in the direction of the magnetic field lines.

Therefore, the correct model is:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Vertical conductor with current flowing up

choices.content: ![Current flows up. Magnetic field lines are circles around the conductor. To the right of the conductor, the magnetic field lines point into the screen.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8fa2fa2067aef09a19f6367c2cb73824.svg)

![Current flows up. Magnetic field lines are circles around the conductor. To the left of the conductor, the magnetic field lines point into the screen.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/59653ed1c6383ba40eb129c4adb3c4cc.svg)

![Current flows up. Magnetic field lines are circles next to the conductor. To the left of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate counterclockwise. To the right of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate clockwise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/52ee700bb80e1d1e8ce8f602a0917ccc.svg)

![Current flows up. Magnetic field lines are circles next to the conductor. To the left of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate clockwise. To the right of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate counterclockwise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c85b6b5b3da7e6a197e63a295792fa6b.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current flows through a straight conductor, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which model correctly shows the shape of the magnetic field lines produced around the conductor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The magnetic field lines around a current-carrying wire form concentric circles *around* the conductor.

The direction of the magnetic field lines is determined using the *right hand rule*. 

If you point the thumb of your *right hand* in the direction of the current (down), your four fingers wrap in the direction of the magnetic field lines.

Therefore, the correct model is:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Vertical conductor with current flowing down

choices.content: ![Current flows down. Magnetic field lines are circles around the conductor. To the right of the conductor, the magnetic field lines point into the screen.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/adf28960efe89f75bd299f5e08a5804a.svg)

![Current flows down. Magnetic field lines are circles around the conductor. To the left of the conductor, the magnetic field lines point into the screen.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/290b243ab0b9d3d344035b6352daf51b.svg)

![Current flows down. Magnetic field lines are circles next to the conductor. To the left of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate counterclockwise. To the right of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate clockwise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1e042057513ee6638de79926911ba1ac.svg)

![Current flows down. Magnetic field lines are circles next to the conductor. To the left of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate clockwise. To the right of the conductor, the magnetic field lines circulate counterclockwise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8a119c7df95fa27c0ea143f3531e7c6f.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current flows through a conductor. As a result, a magnetic field is produced around the conductor.

**Complete the statement.**

* If the amount of current increases, but the direction of current does not change, then the magnetic field around the conductor $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: becomes stronger
becomes weaker
reverses direction
does not change

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current flows through a conductor. As a result, a magnetic field is produced around the conductor.

**Complete the statement.**

* If the amount of current decreases, but the direction of current does not change, then the magnetic field around the conductor $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: becomes stronger
becomes weaker
reverses direction
does not change

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current flows through a straight conductor, as modeled below. Four positions below the conductor are labelled.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Rank the positions by the strength of the magnetic field at each position.**

weaker magnetic field

[[☃ sorter 1]]

stronger magnetic field
hints: The strength of the magnetic field around a current-carrying conductor depends on *distance from the conductor*.

Magnetic field strength is *inversely* related to distance. The greater the distance from the conductor, the weaker the magnetic field.

So, to rank the positions by magnetic field strength, we must consider each position's distance from the conductor.

Position C is farthest from the conductor. So, the magnetic field is weakest there.

We can move closer to the conductor one position at a time to determine the rest of the ranking.

Therefore:

weaker magnetic field

position C

position D

position A

position B

stronger magnetic field

options.alt: A horizontal conductor with a current flowing to the right. Position A is second-closest to the conductor. Position B is closest to the conductor. Position C is farthest from the conductor. Position D is second-farthest from the conductor.

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current flows through a straight conductor, as modeled below. Four positions above the conductor are labelled.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Rank the positions by the strength of the magnetic field at each position.**

stronger magnetic field

[[☃ sorter 1]]

weaker magnetic field
hints: The strength of the magnetic field around a current-carrying conductor depends on *distance from the conductor*.

Magnetic field strength is *inversely* related to distance. The greater the distance from the conductor, the weaker the magnetic field.

So, to rank the positions by magnetic field strength, we must consider each position's distance from the conductor.

Position B is closest to the conductor. So, the magnetic field is strongest there.

We can move farther from the conductor one position at a time to determine the rest of the ranking.

Therefore:

stronger magnetic field

position B

position D

position A

position C

weaker magnetic field

options.alt: A horizontal conductor with a current flowing to the left. Position A is second-farthest from the conductor. Position B is closest to the conductor. Position C is farthest from the conductor. Position D is second-closest to the conductor.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the explanation.**

* A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field. This happens because when current is flowing, $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: many charged particles in the conductor are moving in the same direction
previously neutral particles in the conductor develop an electric charge
the conductor becomes warmer due to increased particle collisions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is a magnetic field produced around a conductor when current flows through it?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Many charged particles in the conductor are moving in the same direction.
Previously neutral particles in the conductor develop an electric charge.
The conductor becomes warmer due to increased particle collisions.
